## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

## plugin:personal-os:notebooklm-mcp
NotebookLM MCP - Access NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com).

**Auth:** If you get authentication errors, run `nlm login` via your Bash/terminal tool. This is the automated authentication method that handles everything. Only use save_auth_tokens as a fallback if the CLI fails.
**Account Switching:** To switch Google Accounts for the MCP server, run `nlm login switch <profile>` in Bash. The MCP server instantly uses the active default profile.
**Confirmation:** Tools with confirm param require user approval before setting confirm=True.
**Studio:** After creating audio/video/infographic/slides, poll studio_status for completion.

Consolidated tools:
- source_add(source_type=url|text|drive|file, url=..., document_id=..., text=..., file_path=...): Add any source type
- studio_create(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Create any artifact type
- studio_revise: Revise individual slides in an existing slide deck
- download_artifact(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Download any artifact type
- note_create/note_list/note_update/note_delete: Manage notes in notebooks

It's the answer to VAIC 2026 problem #135 (sponsor: National 6 Innovation Center). 7 8 But the *way* it's built is the interesting part. This is an **AI agent data platform**. 9 This page explains what that means in one read. For the how, see 10 [`ARCHITECTURE.md`](ARCHITECTURE.md), [`AGENT_FLOWS.md`](AGENT_FLOWS.md), and 11 [`DESIGN_SYSTEM.md`](DESIGN_SYSTEM.md). 12 13 ## What is an "AI agent data platform"? 14 15 A normal data pipeline is code that moves and transforms data through fixed steps. That 16 works when every step is a *precise* rule. It breaks the moment a step needs **judgment** — 17 "read this company's website and figure out what sector it's in," "explain why these two 18 organizations should talk," "write a warm email that invents no facts." 19 20 An **AI agent data platform** solves this by splitting the work in two: 21 22 | The **spine** (deterministic code) | The **agents** (AI) | 23 |---|---| 24 | Owns all data, order, and side effects | Do the fuzzy, judgment-heavy work | 25 | Never guesses; always correct | Never touch the database or the "truth" | 26 | Postgres, queues, retries, leases | Web research, extraction, ranking, writing | 27 | The part you can *trust* | The part that's *smart* | 28 29 The two meet at one narrow, guarded seam: the spine hands an agent a prompt, the agent 30 replies with a **single block of validated JSON**, and the spine checks it against a schema 31 before it's allowed to change anything. If the agent misbehaves, the JSON fails validation 32 and the spine simply retries — nothing incorrect ever lands. 33 34 ```mermaid 35 flowchart LR 36 subgraph spine["🧠 SPINE — deterministic code (the manager)"] 37 direction TB 38 SUP["Supervisor
assigns work, retries, tracks cost"] 39 DB[("Postgres
the single source of truth")] 40 SUP <--> DB 41 end 42 43 subgraph agents["🤖 AGENTS — AI workers (the thinkers)"] 44 direction TB 45 FEY["feynman
crawl the web"] 46 PI["pi
rank · draft · verify"] 47 end 48 49 SUP -->|"prompt"| agents 50 agents -->|"one validated JSON block"| CHECK{"schema
valid?"} 51 CHECK -->|yes| DB 52 CHECK -->|no| SUP 53 ``` 54 55 > **The one-line mental model:** the agents are stateless *workers* doing the thinking; 56 > the spine is the *manager* that remembers everything, assigns work, and refuses to 57 > accept sloppy output. Postgres is the source of truth — the agents just visit. 58 59 ## What it actually does 60 61 Two automated workflows ("sagas"), no human in the loop: 62 63 ```mermaid 64 flowchart LR 65 name["a company
name"] --> e1 66 67 subgraph onboard["1 · Onboarding — name → sourced profile"] 68 direction LR 69 e1["enrich 🤖
crawl the web"] --> e2["extract 🤖
normalize"] --> e3["link 🧠
save + connect"] 70 end 71 72 e3 --> ready(["profile ready
every fact has a source"]) 73 ready --> o1 74 75 subgraph outreach["2 · Outreach — startup → verified intros"] 76 direction LR 77 o1["filter 🧠
narrow"] --> o2["match 🤖
rank + explain"] --> o3["draft 🤖
write EN/VI email"] --> o4["verify 🤖
fact-check"] 78 end 79 80 o4 --> done(["ranked matches
+ verified bilingual drafts"]) 81 ``` 82 83 1. **Onboarding** — turn a bare company name into a rich, *sourced* profile. 84 `enrich` (an agent crawls the real web) → `extract` (an agent normalizes it) → `link` 85 (code saves it + records who's connected to whom). **Every fact carries the URL it came 86 from**; anything the agent can't find is marked "unavailable" rather than invented. 87 88 2. **Outreach** — turn a startup into ranked, ready-to-send introductions. 89 `filter` (code narrows the field) → `match` (an agent ranks partners and explains each 90 fit) → `draft` (an agent writes the bilingual email) → `verify` (a second agent 91 fact-checks the draft and rejects anything hallucinated). 92 93 The result, viewable on a live dashboard: each startup with its top partner matches, a 94 fit score, a bilingual rationale, a verified EN/VI draft email, and a source link behind 95 every claim. 96 97 ## Why build it this way? 98 99 - **Trust.** Nothing that must be correct depends on an AI behaving. Every generated claim 100 is either grounded in a cited source or thrown away. 101 - **Cheap and observable.** The whole reference run — 57 organizations, 125 matches — 102 cost **$1.67**, and every cent is tracked per agent turn in a live cost ledger. 103 - **Recoverable.** If a worker crashes mid-task, the spine notices, requeues the work, and 104 a fresh worker picks it up. Re-running anything is safe (it never duplicates). 105 - **Swappable brains.** Ranking sits behind a plug-in "port" — today it's an LLM judge; 106 it can become embeddings or graph-based matching later with a one-line config change, 107 no rewrite. 108 109 ## The 30-second tour of the code 110 111 | You want to know… | Look at | 112 |---|---| 113 | The big picture, layers, data model | [`ARCHITECTURE.md`](ARCHITECTURE.md) | 114 | How the agents and the two sagas actually run | [`AGENT_FLOWS.md`](AGENT_FLOWS.md) | 115 | The dashboard's look & feel | [`DESIGN_SYSTEM.md`](DESIGN_SYSTEM.md) | 116 | The complete design of record | [`superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`](superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md) | 117 | How to run it from zero | [`../README.md`](../README.md) | 118 119 ## In one breath 120 121 > A deterministic Python spine backed by Postgres orchestrates isolated AI agent processes 122 > that crawl, extract, rank, draft, and verify — turning a list of company names into 123 > sourced profiles and verified bilingual introductions, fully automatically, for about 124 > three cents per organization. 125

1 # Architecture 2 3 Deal-flow Matchmaker (problem #135, VAIC 2026 · NIC). This is the readable companion to 4 the full design of record in 5 [`superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`](superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md). 6 Where they disagree, the spec wins. 7 8 ## 1. The one idea 9 10 > A **deterministic Python spine** owns all state, orchestration, and I/O. Every **fuzzy** 11 > task (web research, extraction, ranking, drafting, verification) is pushed out to an 12 > **isolated LLM agent** that speaks a strict JSON contract. Postgres is the source of 13 > truth; the spine is a **reconciler**, not the record. 14 15 Nothing that must be correct depends on a model behaving. Nothing that must be smart is 16 hard-coded. The seam between the two is a validated JSON block. 17 18 ## 2. Layer map 19 20 ```mermaid 21 flowchart TB 22 subgraph clients["Clients"] 23 CLI["scripts/bootstrap.py · scripts/outreach.py"] 24 DASH["dashboard/index.html
(polls every 3s)"] 25 end 26 27 subgraph api["Read/Control API — api/app.py (FastAPI)"] 28 EP["GET /entities /matches /sagas /jobs
/events /workers /metrics /costs
POST /runs (enqueue only)"] 29 end 30 31 subgraph spine["The Spine (deterministic Python)"] 32 SUP["Supervisor — reconciler loop
spine/supervisor.py"] 33 SAGA["Sagas: onboarding + outreach
spine/sagas.py · spine/outreach.py"] 34 MATCH["Matcher port + rule-filter
spine/matcher/*"] 35 STORE["Store — all SQL
spine/store.py"] 36 TRANS["RPC transport + launcher
spine/transport.py"] 37 end 38 39 subgraph agents["Agent runtimes (isolated processes, stdio JSON-RPC)"] 40 FEY["feynman — crawler runtime
enrich · extract (web tools)"] 41 PI["pi — reasoning runtime
match · draft · verify"] 42 end 43 44 PG[("Postgres 16
entities · edges · artifacts · matches
jobs · events · saga_instances
workers · llm_usage")] 45 46 CLI --> STORE 47 DASH --> EP 48 EP --> STORE 49 SUP --> SAGA --> STORE 50 SUP --> MATCH 51 SUP --> TRANS --> FEY & PI 52 STORE <--> PG 53 SUP <--> PG 54 ``` 55 56 **Rule of the boundary:** the API never touches agents — `POST /runs` only *enqueues*. 57 A running `python -m spine.supervisor` is what actually advances work. This keeps the 58 read surface cheap and side-effect-free, and makes the agent spend observable and bounded 59 in exactly one place. 60 61 ## 3. Components 62 63 | Component | File | Responsibility | 64 |---|---|---| 65 | **Supervisor** | `spine/supervisor.py` | Reconciler: adopt/orphan-kill on boot, lease + dispatch ready jobs under a global concurrency cap, reclaim expired leases. Owns all agent spawning + telemetry. | 66 | **Sagas** | `spine/sagas.py`, `spine/outreach.py` | The two workflows as stage DAGs. Build prompts, validate agent output against `schemas/*.json`, persist artifacts, advance the DAG transactionally. | 67 | **Matcher port** | `spine/matcher/__init__.py` | `Matcher` protocol (pluggable ranking, R12) + the deterministic permissive `rule_filter` (R2) + bilingual sector normalization. | 68 | **LlmJudgeMatcher** | `spine/matcher/llm_judge.py` | v1 Matcher: one `pi` call ranks + explains the filtered handful. No embeddings. | 69 | **Store** | `spine/store.py` | Every SQL statement. Leasing, idempotent upserts, event append, projections, cost ledger, `LISTEN/NOTIFY`. | 70 | **Transport** | `spine/transport.py` | `RpcChannel` (one prompt round-trip over stdio JSON-RPC), `LocalProcessLauncher`, `AgentSpec`. Knows the pi/feynman protocol quirks. | 71 | **Agent registry** | `agents/specs.yaml` | Declarative: which runtime/skill/tools/pool serve each stage. Model **pinned** to `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash`. | 72 | **API** | `api/app.py` | FastAPI read model + `POST /runs`. Serves the dashboard. | 73 74 ## 4. Why a spine + agents (the locked decisions) 75 76 | Decision | Choice | Reason | 77 |---|---|---| 78 | State ownership | Spine + Postgres, never the agent | Agents are stateless workers; nothing correct rides on model memory. | 79 | Agent isolation | Separate OS process per job (N=1) | No warm-context bleed across targets (R7); crash contained to one job. | 80 | Two runtimes | `feynman` = crawler (web tools), `pi` = reasoner (cheap, no web) | Match the tool surface to the task; keep the expensive web runtime off ranking/drafting. | 81 | Orchestration | Postgres-as-log sagas, **no message queue** | One dependency. `LISTEN/NOTIFY` wakes the scheduler; a poll fallback covers missed notifies. | 82 | Recovery | Forward-recovery only, no compensations | Re-run a failed stage; idempotency makes that safe. Simpler than sagas-with-rollback. | 83 | Ranking | Behind a `Matcher` port | Swap `LlmJudgeMatcher` → `EmbeddingMatcher`/`GraphRagMatcher` later with zero saga change. | 84 85 ## 5. Data model at a glance 86 87 Three migrations, three concerns: 88 89 - **`001_core.sql` — domain.** `entities` (unified startups + partners, provenance-rich 90 JSONB profile), `edges` (relationship graph, captured from day 1, consumed by future 91 GraphRAG), `artifacts` (raw agent outputs / blackboard + audit trail), `matches` 92 (ranked + drafted results). 93 - **`002_orchestration.sql` — workflow.** `jobs` (the queue, leased), `events` 94 (append-only log = source of truth), `saga_instances` (folded projection, rebuildable 95 from events). 96 - **`003_control_plane.sql` — operations.** `workers` (registry for orphan detection), 97 `llm_usage` (one row per agent turn → the cost ledger behind `GET /costs`). 98 99 The invariants that make the spine safe live in the schema itself: 100 101 | Constraint | Guarantee | Rule | 102 |---|---|---| 103 | `entities.id = {type}:{slug(name)}` | Deterministic ids → re-running bootstrap/link never duplicates | R9 | 104 | `UNIQUE(artifacts: saga_id, step, target_id)` | Latest-wins upsert; a reclaimed/double-run job can't fork rows | R6 | 105 | `UNIQUE(jobs: saga_id, stage, target_id)` | Per-match idempotency key; guards double-dispatch | R5 | 106 | `events UNIQUE(saga_id, seq)` | Ordered, gap-checked event log | — | 107 | `jobs.lease_expires_at` | Dead worker's lease expires → reclaim loop requeues | R6 | 108 109 ## 6. Reliability model 110 111 The spine is a reconciler over desired + leased state, so failure handling is uniform: 112 113 - **Crash recovery.** A job is leased with an expiry (`LEASE_SECONDS`). A worker that dies 114 mid-job never renews; the reclaim loop flips it back to `ready` and a replacement picks 115 it up. `RpcChannel.close()` SIGKILLs the whole child **process group** so a feynman 116 shim's node child can't keep spending after we let go. 117 - **No double effects.** The two `UNIQUE` keys above mean a re-run produces the same row, 118 not a second one. 119 - **Bounded retries → dead-letter.** A retryable sub-job (the draft↔verify loop) that 120 exhausts `MAX_ATTEMPTS` is marked `dead` with a `DraftDeadLettered` milestone (R5). 121 - **Drain semantics.** `--drain` stops once no job for either saga is `ready`/`leased`. 122 Safe because `finish_stage` enqueues the next stage in the *same transaction* that 123 completes the current one — there is no zero-work window mid-saga. 124 125 `LEASE_SECONDS > AGENT_TIMEOUT` must hold (R6), or a slow-but-alive job gets reclaimed 126 out from under itself. 127 128 ## 7. Observability & cost 129 130 - **Tracing.** Every row carries a `trace_id` (R10); structured logs bind 131 `job_id/saga_id/trace_id/stage` per dispatch (`spine/telemetry.py`). 132 - **Cost ledger.** Each agent turn's `usage.cost` (a nested object on this host, summed to 133 a scalar) is written to `llm_usage`. `GET /costs` and the dashboard header surface total 134 spend, cost/entity, cost/match live. Reference cold-boot run: **$1.67 for 57 entities + 135 125 matches** (R14). 136 - **Guardrails (deliberately light).** Model pinned; `MAX_CONCURRENCY` caps parallel 137 spend; per-turn `AGENT_TIMEOUT`; retry budget `MAX_ATTEMPTS`. 138 139 ## 8. Deployment shape 140 141 - `docker compose up -d postgres` — Postgres 16, migrations apply on init; `bootstrap.py` 142 also runs an idempotent migration runner. 143 - The **supervisor and agent pools always run on the host** (`LocalProcessLauncher`, 144 host `~/.pi` / `~/.feynman` auth). Agents are real local CLIs. 145 - The `api` service is containerized (`api/Dockerfile`) but optional. 146 - **WIP seam:** `ContainerLauncher` reuses the same `RpcChannel` to run a runtime in a 147 container later — designed, not built (spec §12). 148 149 See the [README](../README.md) for the exact cold-boot command sequence, and 150 [`AGENT_FLOWS.md`](AGENT_FLOWS.md) for how a single entity travels through both sagas. 151

match · draft · verify"] end PG[("Postgres 16
entities · edges · artifacts · matches
jobs · events · saga_instances
workers · llm_usage")] CLI --> STORE DASH --> EP EP --> STORE SUP --> SAGA --> STORE SUP --> MATCH SUP --> TRANS --> FEY & PI STORE <--> PG SUP <--> PG ``` **Rule of the boundary:** the API never touches agents — `POST /runs` only *enqueues*. A running `python -m spine.supervisor` is what actually advances work. This keeps the read surface cheap and side-effect-free, and makes the agent spend observable and bounded in exactly one place. ## 3. Components | Component | File | Responsibility | |---|---|---| | **Supervisor** | `spine/supervisor.py` | Reconciler: adopt/orphan-kill on boot, lease + dispatch ready jobs under a global concurrency cap, reclaim expired leases. Owns all agent spawning + telemetry. | | **Sagas** | `spine/sagas.py`, `spine/outreach.py` | The two workflows as stage DAGs. Build prompts, validate agent output against `schemas/*.json`, persist artifacts, advance the DAG transactionally. | | **Matcher port** | `spine/matcher/__init__.py` | `Matcher` protocol (pluggable ranking, R12) + the deterministic permissive `rule_filter` (R2) + bilingual sector normalization. | | **LlmJudgeMatcher** | `spine/matcher/llm_judge.py` | v1 Matcher: one `pi` call ranks + explains the filtered handful. No embeddings. | | **Store** | `spine/store.py` | Every SQL statement. Leasing, idempotent upserts, event append, projections, cost ledger, `LISTEN/NOTIFY`. | | **Transport** | `spine/transport.py` | `RpcChannel` (one prompt round-trip over stdio JSON-RPC), `LocalProcessLauncher`, `AgentSpec`. Knows the pi/feynman protocol quirks. | | **Agent registry** | `agents/specs.yaml` | Declarative: which runtime/skill/tools/pool serve each stage. Model **pinned** to `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash`. | | **API** | `api/app.py` | FastAPI read model + `POST /runs`. Serves the dashboard. | ## 4. Why a spine + agents (the locked decisions) | Decision | Choice | Reason | |---|---|---| | State ownership | Spine + Postgres, never the agent | Agents are stateless workers; nothing correct rides on model memory. | | Agent isolation | Separate OS process per job (N=1) | No warm-context bleed across targets (R7); crash contained to one job. | | Two runtimes | `feynman` = crawler (web tools), `pi` = reasoner (cheap, no web) | Match the tool surface to the task; keep the expensive web runtime off ranking/drafting. | | Orchestration | Postgres-as-log sagas, **no message queue** | One dependency. `LISTEN/NOTIFY` wakes the scheduler; a poll fallback covers missed notifies. | | Recovery | Forward-recovery only, no compensations | Re-run a failed stage; idempotency makes that safe. Simpler than sagas-with-rollback. | | Ranking | Behind a `Matcher` port | Swap `LlmJudgeMatcher` → `EmbeddingMatcher`/`GraphRagMatcher` later with zero saga change. | ## 5. Data model at a glance Three migrations, three concerns: - **`001_core.sql` — domain.** `entities` (unified startups + partners, provenance-rich JSONB profile), `edges` (relationship graph, captured from day 1, consumed by future GraphRAG), `artifacts` (raw agent outputs / blackboard + audit trail), `matches` (ranked + drafted results). - **`002_orchestration.sql` — workflow.** `jobs` (the queue, leased), `events` (append-only log = source of truth), `saga_instances` (folded projection, rebuildable from events). - **`003_control_plane.sql` — operations.** `workers` (registry for orphan detection), `llm_usage` (one row per agent turn → the cost ledger behind `GET /costs`). The invariants that make the spine safe live in the schema itself: | Constraint | Guarantee | Rule | |---|---|---| | `entities.id = {type}:{slug(name)}` | Deterministic ids → re-running bootstrap/link never duplicates | R9 | | `UNIQUE(artifacts: saga_id, step, target_id)` | Latest-wins upsert; a reclaimed/double-run job can't fork rows | R6 | | `UNIQUE(jobs: saga_id, stage, target_id)` | Per-match idempotency key; guards double-dispatch | R5 | | `events UNIQUE(saga_id, seq)` | Ordered, gap-checked event log | — | | `jobs.lease_expires_at` | Dead worker's lease expires → reclaim loop requeues | R6 | ## 6. Reliability model The spine is a reconciler over desired + leased state, so failure handling is uniform: - **Crash recovery.** A job is leased with an expiry (`LEASE_SECONDS`). A worker that dies mid-job never renews; the reclaim loop flips it back to `ready` and a replacement picks it up. `RpcChannel.close()` SIGKILLs the whole child **process group** so a feynman shim's node child can't keep spending after we let go. - **No double effects.** The two `UNIQUE` keys above mean a re-run produces the same row, not a second one. - **Bounded retries → dead-letter.** A retryable sub-job (the draft↔verify loop) that exhausts `MAX_ATTEMPTS` is marked `dead` with a `DraftDeadLettered` milestone (R5). - **Drain semantics.** `--drain` stops once no job for either saga is `ready`/`leased`. Safe because `finish_stage` enqueues the next stage in the *same transaction* that completes the current one — there is no zero-work window mid-saga. `LEASE_SECONDS > AGENT_TIMEOUT` must hold (R6), or a slow-but-alive job gets reclaimed out from under itself. ## 7. Observability & cost - **Tracing.** Every row carries a `trace_id` (R10); structured logs bind `job_id/saga_id/trace_id/stage` per dispatch (`spine/telemetry.py`). - **Cost ledger.** Each agent turn's `usage.cost` (a nested object on this host, summed to a scalar) is written to `llm_usage`. `GET /costs` and the dashboard header surface total spend, cost/entity, cost/match live. Reference cold-boot run: **$1.67 for 57 entities + 125 matches** (R14). - **Guardrails (deliberately light).** Model pinned; `MAX_CONCURRENCY` caps parallel spend; per-turn `AGENT_TIMEOUT`; retry budget `MAX_ATTEMPTS`. ## 8. Deployment shape - `docker compose up -d postgres` — Postgres 16, migrations apply on init; `bootstrap.py` also runs an idempotent migration runner. - The **supervisor and agent pools always run on the host** (`LocalProcessLauncher`, host `~/.pi` / `~/.feynman` auth). Agents are real local CLIs. - The `api` service is containerized (`api/Dockerfile`) but optional. - **WIP seam:** `ContainerLauncher` reuses the same `RpcChannel` to run a runtime in a container later — designed, not built (spec §12). See the [README](../README.md) for the exact cold-boot command sequence, and [`AGENT_FLOWS.md`](AGENT_FLOWS.md) for how a single entity travels through both sagas.

A `success:false` ACK (e.g. missing 68 API key) is surfaced as an error instead of hanging. 69 - **Completion** = `agent_settled` (pi) *or* `agent_end{willRetry:false}` + a short settle 70 grace (feynman never emits `agent_settled`). 71 - Assistant text lives in `turn_end.message.content[].text`; **the terminal message must 72 be a single fenced ` ```json ` block** — parsed by `extract_json_block`, validated 73 against `schemas/.json`. status=seeded"] --> enrich 90 enrich["enrich (feynman)
web research → profile + relationships
every field {value, source_url, confidence}"] --> extract 91 extract["extract (feynman)
normalize sectors (R2)
infer looking_for, stage, desc EN/VI"] --> link 92 link["link (code, no LLM)
merge artifacts → upsert entity status=ready
write relationships to edges (R11)"] --> ready(["EntityReady"]) 93 ``` 94 95 - **enrich** gathers *real* public data from multiple sources. permissive rule-filter (R2)
→ capped candidate handful"] --> match 117 match["match (pi, Matcher port R12)
LlmJudgeMatcher ranks + explains

(scores + rationale)"] 161 MT --> D["draft"] --> DR["matches.draft_en/vi"] 162 DR --> V["verify"] --> ST["matches.status=draft_ready"] 163 ``` 164 165 Each stage reads its predecessors' artifacts from Postgres and writes its own back. 166 Because artifacts upsert latest-wins on the unique key, re-running a stage overwrites 167 rather than forks — which is what makes crash recovery and idempotency free. 168 169 ## 6. 1	# Deal-flow Matchmaker
2	
3	Agent data platform for deal-flow matchmaking (problem #135, VAIC 2026 · sponsor NIC).
4	
5	A **deterministic Python spine** (supervisor + Postgres) owns storage, orchestration
6	(event-sourced sagas), a pluggable ranking `Matcher` port, and all I/O. The fuzzy work —
7	enrichment, extraction, match explanation, drafting, verification — runs inside isolated
8	**`feynman` / `pi`** agent runtimes driven over stdio JSON-RPC. See
9	`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md` for the full design.
10	
11	## Documentation
12	
13	- [`docs/OVERVIEW.md`](docs/OVERVIEW.md) — **start here** — plain-language "what is an AI agent data platform" and what this does
14	- [`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) — layer map, components, data model, reliability & cost model
15	- [`docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md`](docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md) — the runtimes, the spine↔agent JSON contract, and both sagas end to end
16	- [`docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md`](docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md) — dashboard visual language (tokens, components, conventions)
17	- [`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`](docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md) — full design of record
18	
19	## Prerequisites
20	
21	- [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) (Python 3.12+)
22	- Docker + Docker Compose (Postgres 16)
23	- Host agent auth for the pooled runtimes: `~/.pi` and `~/.feynman` (a DeepSeek key —
24	  `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` is the pinned model). The transport lifts
25	  `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` out of host auth if it isn't already exported.
26	
27	Agent-driven stages cost real LLM calls. The supervisor is pinned to `deepseek-v4-flash`
28	with a global concurrency cap (`MAX_CONCURRENCY`) to bound spend.
29	
30	## Configuration
31	
32	Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and adjust:
33	
34	| Var | Default | Purpose |
35	|-----|---------|---------|
36	| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@localhost:5432/dealflow` | Postgres DSN |
37	| `AGENT_MODEL` | `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | Pinned model (all agents) |

file.content:
# Deal-flow Matchmaker

Agent data platform for deal-flow matchmaking (problem #135, VAIC 2026 · sponsor NIC).

A **deterministic Python spine** (supervisor + Postgres) owns storage, orchestration
(event-sourced sagas), a pluggable ranking `Matcher` port, and all I/O. The fuzzy work —
enrichment, extraction, match explanation, drafting, verification — runs inside isolated
**`feynman` / `pi`** agent runtimes driven over stdio JSON-RPC. See
`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md` for the full design.

## Documentation

- [`docs/OVERVIEW.md`](docs/OVERVIEW.md) — **start here** — plain-language "what is an AI agent data platform" and what this does
- [`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) — layer map, components, data model, reliability & cost model
- [`docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md`](docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md) — the runtimes, the spine↔agent JSON contract, and both sagas end to end
- [`docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md`](docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md) — dashboard visual language (tokens, components, conventions)
- [`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`](docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md) — full design of record

## Prerequisites

- [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) (Python 3.12+)
- Docker + Docker Compose (Postgres 16)
- Host agent auth for the pooled runtimes: `~/.pi` and `~/.feynman` (a DeepSeek key —
  `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` is the pinned model). The transport lifts
  `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` out of host auth if it isn't already exported.

Agent-driven stages cost real LLM calls. The supervisor is pinned to `deepseek-v4-flash`
with a global concurrency cap (`MAX_CONCURRENCY`) to bound spend.

## Configuration

Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and adjust:

| Var | Default | Purpose |
|-----|---------|---------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@localhost:5432/dealflow` | Postgres DSN |
| `AGENT_MODEL` | `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | Pinned model (all agents) |
| `MAX_CONCURRENCY` | `4` | Global cap across agent pools |
| `AGENT_TIMEOUT` | `240` | Per-turn wall clock (seconds) |
| `LEASE_SECONDS` | `300` | Job lease window — **keep `> AGENT_TIMEOUT`** (R6) |
| `MAX_ATTEMPTS` | `3` | Retry budget before dead-letter (R5) |

## Quick start (cold boot from zero)

```bash
cp .env.example .env
uv sync

# 1. Postgres (migrations apply via initdb; bootstrap also runs an idempotent runner)
docker compose up -d postgres

# 2. Seed entities + create/enqueue onboarding sagas (idempotent — safe to re-run)
uv run python scripts/bootstrap.py                 # all seed types
#   or a targeted subset:  --types startup --limit 3   |   --names enfarm,Touchstone

# 3. Advance onboarding sagas (enrich -> extract -> link -> EntityReady).
#    --drain exits once the queue empties; omit it to run continuously.
uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain

# 4. Trigger outreach for every onboarded startup (rule-filter -> match -> draft -> verify)
uv run python scripts/outreach.py --all            # or --startup startup:enfarm-agritech

# 5. Advance the outreach sagas
uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain

# 6. API + live dashboard
uv run uvicorn api.app:app --port 8000             # dashboard at http://localhost:8000
```

`POST /runs` (`{"startup_id": ...}` or `{"all": true}`) only **enqueues** outreach sagas —
a supervisor must be running to actually advance them (the API never touches agents). The
dashboard polls every 3s, so a live `python -m spine.supervisor` run is watchable on screen.

The `api` service is also containerized (`docker compose up api` builds `api/Dockerfile`);
the supervisor and agent pools always run on the host via `LocalProcessLauncher`.

## Reset

```bash
docker compose down -v     # drops the pgdata volume — full wipe (R13)
```

## Hardening & recovery (Phase 4)

The spine is a **reconciler**, not the record — Postgres holds desired + leased state.

- **Crash recovery** — a job is leased with an expiry (`LEASE_SECONDS`). A worker that
  dies mid-job never renews its lease; the reclaim loop requeues it (`status='ready'`,
  `leased_by=NULL`) and a replacement picks it up. `close()` SIGKILLs the whole child
  process group so a feynman shim's node child can't keep spending.
- **No double effects** — `UNIQUE(saga_id, step, target_id)` on `artifacts` (latest-wins
  upsert) means a reclaimed/double-run job cannot create a second row;
  `UNIQUE(saga_id, stage, target_id)` on `jobs` (R5) guards double-dispatch.
- **Idempotency** — deterministic entity slugs `{type}:{slug(name)}` (R9) make
  `bootstrap.py` and the `link` stage safe to re-run: no duplicate entities, sagas, jobs,
  or `edges`.
- **Dead-letter** — a retryable sub-job (draft/verify loop) that exhausts `MAX_ATTEMPTS`
  is marked `dead` and a `DraftDeadLettered` milestone is appended (R5).
- **Session hygiene** — pooled workers reset with `{"type":"new_session"}` between jobs
  (R7); the current supervisor goes further, spawning a fresh process per job (N=1) so
  warm context can't bleed across targets.

## Acceptance run (Phase 5 — cold-boot E2E)

The North Star (§9) was verified from a true cold boot (`docker compose down -v`) on the
full seed — 25 startups + 20 investors + 7 corporations + 4 universities + 1 research
institution (57 entities). Exact reproducible path (A5):

```bash
docker compose down -v && docker compose up -d postgres   # 1. clean Postgres
uv run python scripts/bootstrap.py                        # 2. migrate + seed 57 + enqueue
MAX_CONCURRENCY=6 uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain   # 3. onboard (enrich→extract→link)
uv run python scripts/outreach.py --all                   # 4. enqueue outreach for 25 startups
MAX_CONCURRENCY=6 uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain   # 5. filter→match→draft→verify
uv run uvicorn api.app:app --port 8000                    # 6. dashboard at http://localhost:8000
```

Measured results (deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash only, zero dead/failed jobs):

| North Star | Result |
|---|---|
| A1 | 25 startups onboarded; 23 with real `source_url` provenance, fields shaped `{value, source_url, confidence}`, unfound fields `confidence:"unavailable"` (2 sparse-footprint startups per R8) |
| A2 | 32 partners across all 4 types (20 investor / 7 corporation / 4 university / 1 research) with provenance; 654 relationship `edges` |
| A3 | All 25 startups have 5 ranked+explained (EN/VI rationale) + verified bilingual drafts = 125 `draft_ready` matches; cohort spans investor/corporation/university |
| A4 | `GET /entities` (57) → `GET /matches?startup_id=…` (scored, drafted) → dashboard drill-down |
| A5 | The 6 commands above, from zero |

**Cost budget (R14):** the entire run cost **$1.67** — ~$0.029 per onboarded entity,
~$0.013 per match (enrich/feynman dominates at $1.17; pi draft+verify+match ≈ $0.39).
Watch it live at `GET /costs`.

## Tests

```bash
docker compose up -d postgres      # tests need a reachable Postgres (else they skip)
uv run pytest -q
```

Crash-recovery, idempotency, dead-letter, and session-hygiene coverage lives in
`tests/test_crash_recovery.py` and `tests/test_idempotency.py`.

content.text:
I've read the full system. Here's my honest read on your USP question — it's the right question to be stuck on, because right now the app has **two different USPs pointed at two different audiences**, and they aren't the same thing.

## The two candidate USPs you actually have

**1. The engineering USP (what your docs are proud of):**
"An AI agent data platform" — deterministic spine + isolated agents, provenance schema, $1.67 for 57 entities, crash-recoverable, swappable Matcher port. This is genuinely well-built and it impresses *judges and engineers*.

**2. The product USP (what an NIC user actually buys):**
"Connects VN startups with the right partners and drafts the intro." This is what the sponsor's problem #135 asks for.

The trap: your docs lead with #1, but #1 is **infrastructure, not a USP**. A user doesn't care about your reconciler loop. And #2 as stated is *table stakes* — "AI matches startups to investors and writes an email" describes Crunchbase copilots, every accelerator tool, and literally ChatGPT. LLM ranking is not a moat.

## The one thing that is actually defensible

The sharp, honest USP is the intersection of both — the thing a generic "ChatGPT, find me investors" **cannot** claim:

> **Matchmaking you can trust.** Every fact behind every match carries the source URL an agent actually visited; anything it can't find is marked "unavailable" instead of invented; and every draft email is fact-checked by a second agent and *rejected* if it hallucinates.

That's the USP. Not "AI matchmaking" — **verifiable, no-hallucination matchmaking in a trust-sensitive, government-adjacent context.** NIC cannot forward a startup an intro built on made-up facts. You've made "correctness" a visible product feature (source ↗ links, confidence, the verify gate). That's rare and it's the story.

Two supporting differentiators that make it credible, not generic:
- **Vietnam-native + bilingual EN/VI** — shared VN/EN sector normalization, country-aware language choice. A global tool does this badly.
- **The relationship graph you capture from day 1** (`raised_from`, `pilot_with`, `founded_by_alumni_of`, 654 edges) — this is a *sleeping* USP. Nobody's built on it yet, but "who can give me a *warm* intro to X" is a far stronger moat than cold matching, and your schema is already GraphRAG-ready.

## Why this matters for next steps

Your next feature should *prove* the USP you pick, not just add surface area:

- If USP = **trust/provenance** → next step is making verification undeniable: a "trust panel" per match, verifier catch-rate metrics, showing what got *rejected*.
- If USP = **warm intros / graph** → next step is pathfinding over `edges` ("enfarm → shared investor → target corp"), which nothing else in this space does.
- If USP = **the platform itself** → next step is packaging it as reusable agent-data infra, and matchmaking is just the demo.

These lead to very different roadmaps, and it's genuinely your call which bet to make. Let me put the choice to you directly:

options.description: Position as "verifiable matchmaking" — every match sourced, every draft machine-verified. Next steps deepen provenance & the verify gate. Safest bet: builds on what's already your strongest, most-differentiated asset, and fits the NIC/government trust context.

options.label: Warm-intro graph
options.description: Position as "who can introduce you" — pathfinding over the relationship graph you already capture. Next steps build GraphRAG/pathfinding. Highest-ceiling moat, but more to build and the graph edges need to be dense enough to be useful.

options.label: Agent data platform
options.description: Position the reusable spine+agents infra as the product; matchmaking is the reference demo. Impresses engineers/judges, but weakest as a user-facing USP and hardest to sell to NIC.

options.label: Trust + graph combined
options.description: "Verifiable warm-intro matchmaking" — sourced matches AND intro pathfinding. Strongest narrative, but widest scope; risk of doing neither fully in hackathon time.

Use when documenting an architecture decision, writing an ADR, answering "why did we choose Postgres / a relational model / JSONB / event sourcing", or evaluating whether to add a graph DB, vector DB (pgvector), or data warehouse. Forces the decision through storage-paradigm, modeling-approach, mesh-readiness, and table-format frameworks BEFORE anyone writes a migration — so we don't specialize prematurely, and so every choice has a written, revisitable rationale.
- observability-audit: Audit and extend the observability this pipeline ALREADY has (structlog JSON logs + bound correlation ids in spine/telemetry.py). Use when adding metrics or timings, auditing logging/observability, correlating a trace across jobs/sagas, adding span_id, instrumenting the supervisor/sagas/outreach, answering "why is this pipeline slow / where did time go", counting reclaimed leases or dead-letters, or setting MTTR / alert-noise goals. Reach for this BEFORE hand-rolling a new logger, importing OpenTelemetry, or standing up Prometheus/Grafana — this project has none of that and doesn't need it.
- saga-resilience: The reference for hardening the deal-flow spine's saga engine. Read this BEFORE touching spine/supervisor.py, spine/sagas.py, spine/outreach.py, or spine/store.py — or when reasoning about "what happens if an agent hangs / the process dies mid-saga", crash recovery, lease/reclaim/dead-letter, retries and backoff, idempotency and duplicate dispatch, event ordering, or adding a new saga or stage. Maps distributed-systems resilience patterns (saga orchestration, circuit breaker, bulkhead, timeout, retry, idempotency, event sourcing) onto what this single-process + Postgres system ALREADY implements, and tells you what to check before you change it.
- schema-audit: Audit the Postgres schema (entities / edges / artifacts / matches / jobs / events / saga_instances) against data-modeling best practices BEFORE the design ossifies. Use when reviewing or changing the DB schema, adding a table or column, asking "is our schema right", modeling a new entity type / relationship / edge kind, checking constraints / normalization / naming / provenance shape, or planning the graph + vector (GraphRagMatcher / EmbeddingMatcher) phase. This is an AUDIT tool, not a greenfield designer — it checks what already exists in migrations/*.sql and flags issues before they compound across seeded data.

content.text:
Got it — USP = unique selling point, and you've picked **trust / no-hallucination** as the one to lead with. That's the right call: it's already your strongest asset and it's the one thing generic LLM tooling structurally can't claim.

So let me make it concrete. Right now "trust" is *implemented* (provenance schema, verify gate) but it's mostly **invisible and unmeasured**. To make it your USP, the next steps have to make it **visible, measurable, and undeniable**. Here's the roadmap I'd build, in priority order:

## 1. Make trust visible — a "Trust Panel" per match *(highest leverage, lowest effort)*
Right now the source links are buried in field drill-downs. Elevate trust to a first-class UI object on every match:
- **Grounding score** — % of claims in the draft that map to a cited `source_url`.
- **What got rejected** — surface the verify gate's *failures*, not just passes. "Draft rejected 2× for inventing a funding figure → corrected." A tool that shows you what it *refused to send* is dramatically more trustworthy than one that only shows successes.
- **Confidence + freshness** per field (you already store confidence; add "as of" date).

This is the demo moment. When you show NIC "here's the fact, here's the URL, here's the hallucination we caught and killed," you've won the trust argument on screen.

## 2. Make trust measurable — a verification scorecard
Trust claims need numbers or they're marketing. Add aggregate metrics to `/metrics`:
- Verifier catch-rate (drafts rejected / total), avg attempts-to-pass, dead-letter rate.
- Provenance coverage (fields with real `source_url` vs `unavailable`) — you already report 23/25; make it live.
- This turns "we don't hallucinate" into "**X% of drafts fail our fact-check before a human ever sees them, and here's the trend.**"

## 3. Make trust adversarial — harden the verify gate
Your verifier is a single `pi` judge over 4 checks. For a *trust* USP, that's the crown jewel and currently the weakest link (one model, one pass, self-graded). Strengthen it:
- Multi-check or ensemble verification on high-stakes claims (funding, headcount, partnerships — the facts that embarrass you if wrong).
- A **"citation actually supports the claim"** check — right now you verify a `source_url` *exists*, not that the URL *says what the draft claims*. That gap is where trust actually breaks. Closing it is the single most defensible thing you can build.

## 4. Give the user a trust *action* — approve / flag / correct
Trust isn't just display; it's a workflow. A human-in-the-loop review queue where NIC can accept a match, flag a bad fact (feeds back as `verify_feedback`), or request re-enrichment. This is also what turns a hackathon demo into something NIC would actually operate.

---

**My recommendation on sequencing:** do **#1 + #3's citation-support check first**. #1 makes the USP *seen*; the citation-support check makes it *real* (it closes your one genuine hallucination hole). Together they're a tight, honest demo you can build in hackathon time. #2 and #4 are strong follow-ons but lower-urgency.

Want me to turn one of these into an actual implementation plan against your current code (`spine/outreach.py`, the verify stage, `schemas/verify.json`, the dashboard)? If so, I'd start with the **citation-support check** since it's the highest-value and touches the verify contract you already have.

# Deal-flow Matchmaker

Agent data platform for deal-flow matchmaking (problem #135, VAIC 2026 · sponsor NIC).

A **deterministic Python spine** (supervisor + Postgres) owns storage, orchestration
(event-sourced sagas), a pluggable ranking `Matcher` port, and all I/O. The fuzzy work —
enrichment, extraction, match explanation, drafting, verification — runs inside isolated
**`feynman` / `pi`** agent runtimes driven over stdio JSON-RPC. See
`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md` for the full design.

## Documentation

- [`docs/OVERVIEW.md`](docs/OVERVIEW.md) — **start here** — plain-language "what is an AI agent data platform" and what this does
- [`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) — layer map, components, data model, reliability & cost model
- [`docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md`](docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md) — the runtimes, the spine↔agent JSON contract, and both sagas end to end
- [`docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md`](docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md) — dashboard visual language (tokens, components, conventions)
- [`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`](docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md) — full design of record

## Prerequisites

- [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) (Python 3.12+)
- Docker + Docker Compose (Postgres 16)
- Host agent auth for the pooled runtimes: `~/.pi` and `~/.feynman` (a DeepSeek key —
  `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` is the pinned model). The transport lifts
  `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` out of host auth if it isn't already exported.

Agent-driven stages cost real LLM calls. The supervisor is pinned to `deepseek-v4-flash`
with a global concurrency cap (`MAX_CONCURRENCY`) to bound spend.

## Configuration

Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and adjust:

| Var | Default | Purpose |
|-----|---------|---------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@localhost:5432/dealflow` | Postgres DSN |
| `AGENT_MODEL` | `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | Pinned model (all agents) |
| `MAX_CONCURRENCY` | `4` | Global cap across agent pools |
| `AGENT_TIMEOUT` | `240` | Per-turn wall clock (seconds) |
| `LEASE_SECONDS` | `300` | Job lease window — **keep `> AGENT_TIMEOUT`** (R6) |
| `MAX_ATTEMPTS` | `3` | Retry budget before dead-letter (R5) |

## Quick start (cold boot from zero)

```bash
cp .env.example .env
uv sync

# 1. Postgres (migrations apply via initdb; bootstrap also runs an idempotent runner)
docker compose up -d postgres

# 2. Seed entities + create/enqueue onboarding sagas (idempotent — safe to re-run)
uv run python scripts/bootstrap.py                 # all seed types
#   or a targeted subset:  --types startup --limit 3   |   --names enfarm,Touchstone

# 3. Advance onboarding sagas (enrich -> extract -> link -> EntityReady).
#    --drain exits once the queue empties; omit it to run continuously.
uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain

# 4. Trigger outreach for every onboarded startup (rule-filter -> match -> draft -> verify)
uv run python scripts/outreach.py --all            # or --startup startup:enfarm-agritech

# 5. Advance the outreach sagas
uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain

# 6. API + live dashboard
uv run uvicorn api.app:app --port 8000             # dashboard at http://localhost:8000
```

`POST /runs` (`{"startup_id": ...}` or `{"all": true}`) only **enqueues** outreach sagas —
a supervisor must be running to actually advance them (the API never touches agents). The
dashboard polls every 3s, so a live `python -m spine.supervisor` run is watchable on screen.

The `api` service is also containerized (`docker compose up api` builds `api/Dockerfile`);
the supervisor and agent pools always run on the host via `LocalProcessLauncher`.

## Reset

```bash
docker compose down -v     # drops the pgdata volume — full wipe (R13)
```

## Dump & restore

`pg_dump` lives inside the Postgres container, so run it through Compose (no host
Postgres client needed). Restores cleanly anywhere (`--no-owner --no-privileges`):

```bash
# Dump schema + data to a timestamped file
docker compose exec -T postgres \
  pg_dump -U dealflow -d dealflow --no-owner --no-privileges > dump_dealflow_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).sql

# Restore into a running (empty) database
docker compose exec -T postgres psql -U dealflow -d dealflow < dump_dealflow_*.sql
```

Dump files (`dump_*.sql`) are git-ignored — they are data artifacts, not source.

## Hardening & recovery (Phase 4)

The spine is a **reconciler**, not the record — Postgres holds desired + leased state.

- **Crash recovery** — a job is leased with an expiry (`LEASE_SECONDS`). A worker that
  dies mid-job never renews its lease; the reclaim loop requeues it (`status='ready'`,
  `leased_by=NULL`) and a replacement picks it up. `close()` SIGKILLs the whole child
  process group so a feynman shim's node child can't keep spending.
- **No double effects** — `UNIQUE(saga_id, step, target_id)` on `artifacts` (latest-wins
  upsert) means a reclaimed/double-run job cannot create a second row;
  `UNIQUE(saga_id, stage, target_id)` on `jobs` (R5) guards double-dispatch.
- **Idempotency** — deterministic entity slugs `{type}:{slug(name)}` (R9) make
  `bootstrap.py` and the `link` stage safe to re-run: no duplicate entities, sagas, jobs,
  or `edges`.
- **Dead-letter** — a retryable sub-job (draft/verify loop) that exhausts `MAX_ATTEMPTS`
  is marked `dead` and a `DraftDeadLettered` milestone is appended (R5).
- **Session hygiene** — pooled workers reset with `{"type":"new_session"}` between jobs
  (R7); the current supervisor goes further, spawning a fresh process per job (N=1) so
  warm context can't bleed across targets.

## Acceptance run (Phase 5 — cold-boot E2E)

The North Star (§9) was verified from a true cold boot (`docker compose down -v`) on the
full seed — 25 startups + 20 investors + 7 corporations + 4 universities + 1 research
institution (57 entities). Exact reproducible path (A5):

```bash
docker compose down -v && docker compose up -d postgres   # 1. clean Postgres
uv run python scripts/bootstrap.py                        # 2. migrate + seed 57 + enqueue
MAX_CONCURRENCY=6 uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain   # 3. onboard (enrich→extract→link)
uv run python scripts/outreach.py --all                   # 4. enqueue outreach for 25 startups
MAX_CONCURRENCY=6 uv run python -m spine.supervisor --drain   # 5. filter→match→draft→verify
uv run uvicorn api.app:app --port 8000                    # 6. dashboard at http://localhost:8000
```

Measured results (deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash only, zero dead/failed jobs):

| North Star | Result |
|---|---|
| A1 | 25 startups onboarded; 23 with real `source_url` provenance, fields shaped `{value, source_url, confidence}`, unfound fields `confidence:"unavailable"` (2 sparse-footprint startups per R8) |
| A2 | 32 partners across all 4 types (20 investor / 7 corporation / 4 university / 1 research) with provenance; 654 relationship `edges` |
| A3 | All 25 startups have 5 ranked+explained (EN/VI rationale) + verified bilingual drafts = 125 `draft_ready` matches; cohort spans investor/corporation/university |
| A4 | `GET /entities` (57) → `GET /matches?startup_id=…` (scored, drafted) → dashboard drill-down |
| A5 | The 6 commands above, from zero |

**Cost budget (R14):** the entire run cost **$1.67** — ~$0.029 per onboarded entity,
~$0.013 per match (enrich/feynman dominates at $1.17; pi draft+verify+match ≈ $0.39).
Watch it live at `GET /costs`.

## Tests

```bash
docker compose up -d postgres      # tests need a reachable Postgres (else they skip)
uv run pytest -q
```

Crash-recovery, idempotency, dead-letter, and session-hygiene coverage lives in
`tests/test_crash_recovery.py` and `tests/test_idempotency.py`.

1. **AI matching you can trust — the exact, most-correct result** — framed around your grounding + verify-gate advantage so "correct" is backed by evidence, not just asserted.
